1: "The LORD was gracious to Sarah as he had said (kaʾăšer ʾāmar)... what he had promised (kaʾăšer dibbēr)" — the double-confirmation of fulfillment (as he said / as he promised) is the narrative's deliberate emphasis on covenant faithfulness. The two phrases distinguish promise (dibbēr, speech/word) and statement (ʾāmar, saying/pronouncement) — both the formal promise (Genesis 17:19) and the informal announcement (Genesis 18:10) are fulfilled. YHWH's word-faithfulness is the verse's theological center: the promise made, the promise kept, without remainder or deviation.
2: "At the very time God had promised him (lammôʿēd ʾăšer dibber ʾittô ʾĕlōhîm)" — the môʿēd (appointed time/set time) formula of 17:21 and 18:14 is fulfilled exactly. The covenant-heir arrives at the precisely appointed moment — the timing is as theologically significant as the fact. In the ANE, a promise fulfilled at the stated time demonstrated divine reliability in a way that delayed or approximate fulfillment would not. "In his old age" — the age notation reinforces the miraculous character: the child born to a hundred-year-old father and ninety-year-old mother is incontrovertibly the product of divine intervention, not of biological expectation.
3: "Abraham gave the name Isaac (yiṣḥāq) to the son Sarah bore him" — following the divine command of Genesis 17:19 ("call him Isaac"). The naming is the third beat in the laughter-trilogy: Abraham laughed incredulously at the promise (Genesis 17:17); Sarah laughed in disbelief at the re-promise (Genesis 18:12); now the child of impossible promise is born and named for the laughter his coming provoked. The name "he laughs" encapsulates the entire narrative arc: incredulity transformed through divine faithfulness into joy. The covenant-heir is named for the human response to the covenant-promise — the laugh of unbelief that became the laugh of delight.
4: "Abraham circumcised him, as God had commanded him" — the covenant-sign applied to the covenant-heir on the eighth day (Genesis 17:12). Isaac's circumcision on the eighth day (as opposed to Ishmael's at thirteen, Genesis 17:25) establishes from birth the covenant-identity marker that distinguishes the covenant-line. The immediate compliance — "Abraham circumcised him" on the stated day — is the narrative's signal that Abraham's covenant-obedience extends to the covenant-heir from the first week of life: the covenant encompasses the child before the child can choose.
5: "Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born" — the chronological summary closes the age-tracking that has run through the patriarchal narrative. Abraham was 75 at the call (Genesis 12:4), 86 at Ishmael's birth (Genesis 16:16), 99 at the covenant-renewal (Genesis 17:1), and 100 at Isaac's birth. The 25-year gap between promise and fulfillment is the covenant's patience — YHWH waited through Abram's Egypt episode, through the Lot-separation, through the Hagar-Ishmael episode, and through the Abimelech episode before fulfilling the primary promise. The covenant is not hurried by human impatience or altered by human failure.
6: Sarah's laughter of joy — "God has brought me laughter (ṣĕḥōq), and everyone who hears about this will laugh with me" — the third and resolving instance of the laughter-root in the Isaac narrative. Abraham's incredulous laugh (Genesis 17:17) and Sarah's disbelieving laugh (Genesis 18:12) are resolved into communal joy: "everyone who hears will laugh with me." The name Isaac embeds all three laughs into the covenant-heir's identity — the one who was conceived amid incredulity is born to communal laughter. The communal joy ("everyone who hears") extends the blessing beyond the immediate family: others participate in the covenant-joy of the impossible birth.
7: "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children (heitîqāh ḇānîm Śārāh)?" — the nursing (sustained lactation) is the physical evidence of fulfillment that exceeds the bare pregnancy. In the ANE medical understanding, sustained nursing demonstrated the biological reality of the birth more definitively than pregnancy alone. The rhetorical wonder-question ("who would have said?") is the standard biblical form for expressing the gap between what was expected and what occurred: "who has believed our report?" (Isaiah 53:1); "who has heard such a thing?" (Isaiah 66:8). The impossibility made possible becomes the paradigm of divine power over natural limitation.
8: The weaning feast marks Isaac's survival of the highest-mortality period of ancient childhood. In the ANE, children were typically weaned between two and three years of age; the weaning feast celebrated the child's survival past the most vulnerable period of infancy. Child mortality in the ancient world was extremely high — estimates suggest 30-50% of children died before age five. Isaac's weaning feast is therefore not merely a social celebration but a survival-milestone: the covenant-heir has survived the most dangerous years of life. Abraham's "great feast (mišteh gādôl)" marks the occasion as a covenant-community celebration.
9: "Sarah saw that the son whom Hagar the Egyptian had borne to Abraham was mocking (mĕṣaḥēq)" — the Piel of ṣāḥaq (to laugh/play/mock) again. The same root as Isaac's name; the same root as the "joking" of Lot's sons-in-law (Genesis 19:14). The exact nature of Ishmael's action is ambiguous: the Piel can mean playing/laughing without hostile intent, or mocking/scorning with hostile intent. The LXX adds "with her son Isaac" (paizonta meta Isaak tou huiou autēs); Paul's citation (Galatians 4:29: "the son born in the ordinary way persecuted the son born by the power of the Spirit") interprets it as persecution. The narrative's deliberate ambiguity invites the reader's discernment without forcing a verdict.
10: Sarah's demand — "the son of the slave woman (ʾāmāh) will never share in the inheritance with my son Isaac" — invokes ANE inheritance law. The Code of Hammurabi (Laws 170-171) distinguishes between the children of a wife and the children of a slave-concubine: if the husband publicly acknowledged the concubine's children, they received a share of the inheritance; but the wife's children took the primary share. Sarah's demand that Ishmael be excluded from the inheritance is a legal claim, not merely an emotional one — she is asserting Isaac's covenantal right as the primary heir against the competing claim of the concubine's son.
11: "The matter distressed Abraham greatly because it concerned his son (ʿal-ʾōdôt bĕnô)" — Ishmael is identified as "his son" in Abraham's emotional response. Abraham has raised Ishmael for over thirteen years (Ishmael was born when Abraham was 86, Isaac when he was 100 — Ishmael is now about 16-17 years old). The affective reality of the expulsion — sending away a teenager he has raised from birth — is the narrative's acknowledgment of the genuine human cost. Abraham is not callous; he is in conflict between covenant-clarity (Isaac is the heir) and parental love (Ishmael is his son).
12: "It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned (kî bĕyiṣḥāq yiqqārēʾ lĕkā zāraʿ)" — literally "through Isaac shall seed be called to you." The divine explanation resolves the conflict by restating the covenant-priority: the covenant-line runs through Isaac, not Ishmael. Paul cites this verse (Romans 9:7; Hebrews 11:18) as the foundational statement of the principle of divine election within the physical lineage — not all of Abraham's physical descendants are the covenant-heirs; the covenant-line is defined by divine calling (yiqqārēʾ, will be called/reckoned) rather than mere biological descent.
13: "I will make the son of the slave woman into a nation also, because he is your offspring (kî zarʿăkā hûʾ)" — the divine commitment to Ishmael is grounded in the Abrahamic biological connection: "because he is your offspring." The covenant does not encompass Ishmael, but the blessing does; the distinction is precisely Paul's argument in Galatians 4:21-31 (the children of the free woman inherit; the children of the slave woman receive blessing but not the inheritance of the covenant). Ishmael's national destiny (twelve princes, Genesis 17:20; Genesis 25:12-18) is the overflow of Abrahamic blessing, not the covenant itself.
14: "Abraham took some food and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar... and sent her off with the boy" — the minimal provision for the desert journey (some food, a single water-skin) is stark. The Beersheba desert is the Negev wilderness south of Canaan — habitable by desert-nomads but dangerous for those without established water sources. The "early next morning" departure parallels Abraham's early-morning departures at moments of covenant-decisive action (Genesis 19:27; Genesis 22:3). The provision, though minimal, is what Abraham could give while obeying the divine command; the narrative does not present him as indifferent to their survival but as constrained by the divine instruction.
15: The dehydration crisis — "when the water in the skin was gone, she put the boy under one of the bushes (taḥat ʾaḥad haśśîḥîm)" — the desert landscape of the Beersheba wilderness. Desert shrubs (siḥ, low desert bushes) provide minimal shade but sufficient shelter for a dying child. The narrative's detail is precise and humanly real: Hagar's response to the water-failure is the care of placing the child under what shelter is available before withdrawing to grieve. The physical details of the desert-survival crisis ground the spiritual crisis in material reality.
16: "I cannot watch the boy die (ʾal-ʾerʾeh bĕmôt hayyelep)" — the maternal grief of the mother who cannot prevent and cannot witness. "She sat about a bowshot away" — the distance of grief: close enough to hear, far enough not to see. "She began to sob (wattiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlāh wattēḇk)" — the lifting of her voice in weeping is the same idiom used for Esau's weeping (Genesis 27:38) and Joseph's weeping (Genesis 45:2; Genesis 46:29). The mother's weeping in the desert, unable to save her child, is one of the OT's most humanly immediate grief-portraits.
17: "God heard the boy crying (wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-qôl hannaʿar)" — the divine hearing activates the child's name: Ishmael means "God hears" (yišmāʿ ʾēl, Genesis 16:11). At the moment of his near-death, the God who named him "God-hears" hears him. The malʾak ʾĕlōhîm (angel of God) rather than the malʾak YHWH of 16:7 appears — the pattern of divine attention to Hagar and Ishmael in the desert is repeated with slightly different divine-name usage, possibly reflecting different source traditions, but the theological content is consistent: YHWH hears the marginalized child in the desert.
18: "For I will make him into a great nation (lĕgôy gādôl)" — the promise given to Hagar in Genesis 16:10 (innumerable descendants) and to Abraham regarding Ishmael in Genesis 17:20 (twelve rulers, a great nation) is now directly given again in the moment of Ishmael's near-death. The promise is reiterated at the point of its apparent dissolution: precisely when the covenant-obligation seems most at risk (the heir of the promise is dying in the desert), YHWH intervenes to fulfill it. The same pattern — promise at the moment of greatest threat — characterizes the entire patriarchal narrative.
19: "Then God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water (bĕʾēr māyim)" — the divine gift of perception of what was already present. The well was presumably there; Hagar's crisis-blindness prevented her from seeing it. Whether the well is a miraculous creation or a miraculous revelation of existing water, the narrative attributes both the seeing and the water to divine provision. The well in the desert as a sign of divine provision becomes one of the OT's signature images of grace in extremity — the same pattern governs Elijah's desert provision (1 Kings 19:5-8) and the wilderness water miracles (Exodus 15:22-25; Genesis 17:1-7; Numbers 20:2-11).
20: "God was with the boy (wayhî ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hannaʿar) as he grew up" — the divine-presence formula applied to Ishmael. The same formula characterizes the covenant-line: YHWH is "with" Isaac (Genesis 26:3,24), Jacob (Genesis 28:15; Genesis 31:3; Genesis 35:3), Joseph (Genesis 39:2,21,23), and Moses. Ishmael receives covenant-presence language even though he is outside the covenant-line — YHWH's presence is not confined to the covenant-holder. "He lived in the desert and became an archer (rōḇeh qešet)" — the archer as the desert-warrior; the wild-donkey description of Genesis 16:12 (free and fierce in the wilderness) is fulfilled in the desert-archer lifestyle. The Desert of Paran becomes Ishmael's territory.
21: "His mother got a wife for him from Egypt (miṣrāyim)" — Hagar the Egyptian secures an Egyptian wife for her son; the marriage pattern connects Ishmael to Egypt (Hagar's homeland) and establishes the Arabian peoples who descend from him as having Egyptian maternal lineage through Ishmael's wife. In the ANE, mothers arranged marriages for their sons (Rebekah's role in Genesis 27:46-28:5; Hagar's role here); the mother's negotiation of the marriage was the standard practice in a patriarchal household where the father's authority was primary but the mother's relational networks were the practical resource for marriage-arrangement.
22: "God is with you in everything you do (ʾĕlōhîm ʿimmĕkā bĕkōl ʾăšer-ʾattāh ʿōśeh)" — Abimelech's observation is a pagan king's theological recognition of the covenant-God's activity through the covenant-partner. The same recognition appears repeatedly in the patriarchal narratives: Pharaoh recognizes the divine spirit in Joseph (Genesis 41:38-39); Laban recognizes that YHWH has blessed him because of Jacob (Genesis 30:27). The divine-presence-that-makes-prosperous (ʾĕlōhîm ʿimmĕkā) is the signature of the covenant-bearer that the surrounding nations observe without necessarily sharing.
23: Abimelech's covenant-request uses the vocabulary of ḥesed — "show to me the same kindness (ḥesed) I have shown to you" — the covenant-loyalty word that characterizes YHWH's relationship to Israel. The pagan king invokes ḥesed as the basis for a treaty: mutual covenant-loyalty between neighboring parties. The request follows the ANE non-aggression treaty format: a mutual oath of ḥesed (covenant-kindness/loyalty) toward each other's descendants "to the country where you now reside as a foreigner (bāʾāreṣ ʾăšer-gartāh bāh)" — Abraham's gēr status in the land is formally acknowledged even as Abimelech seeks a permanent covenant with him.
24: "I swear it (ʾānōkî ʾiššāḇēaʿ)" — Abraham's oath-response to Abimelech's treaty-request is immediate and unqualified. The formal oath (šābaʿ) is the standard ANE treaty-ratification speech-act; the parties commit themselves under divine sanction to the treaty-terms. Abraham's readiness to swear a covenant-loyalty treaty with a pagan king reflects the covenant-patriarch's understanding that the Abrahamic covenant's blessing-to-all-nations (Genesis 12:3) includes covenant-relationships with pagan neighbors, not only with Israel's descendants.
25: The well dispute — Abraham "complained (wayyôkaḥ) about a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized" — the first of several patriarchal well-disputes in the Negev (Isaac's well-disputes at Esek, Sitnah, and Rehoboth, Genesis 26:20-22). Water rights in the semi-arid Negev were the most critical and contentious form of property in the patriarchal economy: wells required significant labor to dig, required ongoing maintenance, and were the essential resource for both human survival and pastoral agriculture. The well-dispute context makes the subsequent treaty (vv27-31) practical as well as political: Beersheba's well is the physical basis for the covenant.
26: Abimelech's denial — "I don't know who did this" — is the standard ANE response of a senior party when challenged about the actions of his subordinates: the king denies personal responsibility for what his servants did without his knowledge. Whether Abimelech's denial is credible or convenient is left ambiguous; Abraham accepts it and proceeds to the covenant-ratification. The practical resolution prioritizes the covenant-relationship (sealing the Beersheba treaty) over the well-grievance (which is satisfied by the gift of seven lambs in v30).
27: "Abraham brought sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a treaty (wayyikrĕtû šĕnêhem bĕrît)" — the covenant-cutting formula. The gift of sheep and cattle precedes the covenant ratification — a covenant gift establishing the treaty-context. This is the first "treaty" (bĕrît) between Abraham and a non-family party; the vocabulary and form are the same as the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 15:18; Genesis 17:2) and foreshadow the Israelite treaty-formula used in political agreements (Joshua 9:6; 1 Samuel 11:1).
28: Abraham "set apart (wayyaṣṣēḇ) seven ewe lambs from the flock" — the deliberate separation of seven lambs from the general treaty-gift (v27) signals a special purpose for these seven. The use of seven in ANE covenant-making is well-documented: the number seven appears in treaty-oaths, covenant sacrifices, and the naming of covenant-participants (cf. seven altars in Numbers 23:1-4,14,29). The seven lambs function as the physical symbol of the oath — the witness-animals that embody the covenant's terms.
29: Abimelech's question — "what is the meaning of these seven ewe lambs you have set apart?" — requires Abraham to articulate the symbolic function of the gift. The question-and-answer format for covenant-symbols is a consistent ANE treaty practice: the parties verbalize the meaning of the symbolic elements to ensure shared understanding of what the symbols represent. Compare the Passover instruction to articulate the meaning of the meal elements (Exodus 12:26-27; Genesis 13:8) when children ask "what does this mean?"
30: "Accept these seven lambs from my hand as a witness (ʿēḏāh) that I dug this well" — the seven lambs serve a double function: they ratify the covenant and they document the well's ownership. In a pre-written-record context, the acceptance of a symbolic gift in the presence of witnesses creates a legal record: Abimelech's acceptance of the seven lambs constitutes public acknowledgment of Abraham's prior claim to the well. The lambs as "witness" (ʿēḏāh) parallels the use of memorial stones as witnesses (Joshua 24:27; Genesis 31:48).
31: Beersheba (bĕʾēr šābaʿ) — the place-name etymology is a double pun: "well of seven (šeḇaʿ)" (referring to the seven lambs) and "well of oath (šĕḇûʿāh)" (referring to the oath sworn there). Both meanings are embedded in the name; the narrative deliberately triggers both. Beersheba becomes one of the primary sacred sites of the patriarchal covenant-journey: Abraham (v33), Isaac (Genesis 26:23-25), and Jacob (Genesis 46:1) all worship YHWH at Beersheba. The later phrase "from Dan to Beersheba" (Judges 20:1; 1 Samuel 3:20; 2 Samuel 3:10; etc.) uses Beersheba as the southern boundary of Israel — the place Abraham named for his covenant with Abimelech.
32: "Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his forces returned to the land of the Philistines" — the formal conclusion of the treaty. The mention of Phicol alongside Abimelech in the treaty-making follows the ANE protocol of having the military commander as well as the king as a treaty-party; the commander's presence ensures the treaty is binding on the military force, not just on the royal office. The "land of the Philistines" anachronism (see v1 note) frames the Gerar territory consistently for the narrative's later audience.
33: "Abraham planted a tamarisk tree (ʾēšel) in Beersheba, and there he called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God (ʾĒl ʿÔlām)" — the divine name ʾĒl ʿÔlām (Eternal God/God of eternity) appears only here in the OT. It identifies YHWH with the Canaanite supreme deity ʾĒl combined with the covenant-word ʿôlām (eternal/everlasting) — the same word characterizing both the Noahic (Genesis 9:16) and Abrahamic (Genesis 17:7) covenants. The tamarisk (ʾēšel) is a long-lived tree (estimated lifespan of hundreds of years) appropriate to a place named for an everlasting covenant; planting it is a permanence-gesture at the treaty-site.
34: "Abraham stayed in the land of the Philistines for a long time (yāmîm rabbîm)" — the extended residence following the Abimelech-treaty brings the Gerar narrative to a close. The "many days" of residence contrasts with the provisional quality of the patriarchal sojourn (gēr) described throughout; Abraham lives in Gerar under the protection of the Abimelech-treaty. The residence also establishes the context for the subsequent Akedah (Genesis 22), which begins with Abraham already in the region and proceeds to Moriah.
